peer_exemplars.reasoning: High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.

Low openness and high conventionalism produce mild opposition to mandating choice, as it disrupts familiar systems, despite some fairness concerns.
High openness and a free-spirit HEXAD profile support user autonomy in algorithmic choice, while moderate libertarian anti-censorship aligns with this, yielding clear support.

Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.
High openness and a free-spirit user type strongly favor user choice, while low agreeableness and anti-domination values reinforce support for mandates that reduce platform control.
Very low openness and low digital literacy lead to near-neutral ambivalence, as the concept is unfamiliar and the person lacks strong opinion on algorithmic choice.
Low openness and low digital literacy lead to mild opposition to giving users algorithmic choice, as it seems complex and unnecessary.
